title: What Does the Event Still Want Me to Know?
subtitle: A journal prompt for the thing that has not been fully processed
excerpt: A journal prompt for getting underneath the event that has not been forgiven. The prompt asks what carrying it is still giving you, what you would lose if you let it go, and what the event still wants you to know.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to understand why you are still carrying something rather than just trying to release it. Not for acute, raw hurt — for the hurt that has been with you long enough to be examined.

content.text: A ten-minute journal prompt for understanding the event that is still with you. Forgiveness that does not come has usually not come for a reason. This prompt asks what that reason is.
New to journal prompts as a practice? Read

Think of the event or the person you have not forgiven. What does carrying this still give you? What would you lose if you let it go? What does the event still want you to know? Think about yourself, what you needed, and what you are still protecting.

Write without editing. The question about what you would lose if you let it go is often the most useful one. Unforgiven events frequently protect something: a boundary, an account of what happened, a version of yourself that was wronged and deserves to be acknowledged. Name what is being protected before trying to put the event down.
